sources.certVol1: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

sourceAudit.note: Content is original/paraphrased from the cited sources; questions are original. Verify command syntax against official Cisco docs.

objectives.title: Determine how a router makes a forwarding decision by default

ckus.title: Longest Prefix Match
ckus.summary: When several routes match a packet’s destination, the router uses the route with the most specific (longest) prefix length, regardless of administrative distance or metric.

sourceRefs.sourceName: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)
sourceRefs.chapter: Ch 16 — IP Forwarding with the Longest Prefix Match

ckus.title: Administrative Distance
ckus.summary: A measure of how trustworthy a routing source is (lower = more trusted). Used to choose between routes to the SAME prefix learned from DIFFERENT sources. An AD of 255 means the route is never installed.

sourceRefs.sourceName: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

ckus.title: Routing Protocol Metric
ckus.summary: A value a routing protocol assigns to a route to rank paths. Metrics are only comparable WITHIN the same protocol (OSPF cost vs EIGRP composite are not comparable); AD decides between protocols first, then metric breaks ties within one.

ckus.title: Default Route (Gateway of Last Resort)
ckus.summary: The route 0.0.0.0/0 (::/0 for IPv6) with prefix length 0 — it matches every destination but is the LEAST specific, so longest-prefix-match only uses it when no more-specific route matches.

sourceRefs.sourceName: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

sourceRefs.notes: Default route is configured under 3.3 but is central to the 3.2 forwarding decision.

tiers.beginner: When a packet arrives, the router looks at the destination IP and checks its routing table for a matching network. If more than one entry matches, it picks the most specific one — the one that pins down the most bits of the address. If two sources (say a static route and OSPF) both offer a route to the exact same network, the router trusts the one with the lower administrative distance. If it is still tied within one routing protocol, the lower metric wins.
tiers.intermediate: A router makes its default forwarding decision in a strict order. (1) Longest prefix match: among all routes whose network/prefix the destination falls inside, the route with the longest prefix length is chosen — this happens FIRST and overrides everything else. (2) Administrative distance: AD only matters when comparing routes to the SAME prefix that were learned from DIFFERENT sources; the lower AD (more trusted source) is installed in the routing table. (3) Metric: when the same routing protocol offers multiple routes to the same prefix, the protocol’s metric breaks the tie (lower is better). A default route (0.0.0.0/0) has prefix length 0, so it is the absolute last resort.
tiers.examReady: Default forwarding logic, in order: longest prefix match → administrative distance → metric. Longest prefix match is the packet-forwarding decision: the router always forwards using the matching route with the longest prefix length, full stop — AD and metric do NOT override a more-specific route. AD and metric instead govern which routes get INSTALLED in the routing table in the first place: when two sources advertise the identical prefix, the lower AD wins (Connected 0, Static 1, EIGRP 90, OSPF 110, RIP 120, External EIGRP 170, 255 = never installed). Metric only compares routes from the same protocol and is not comparable across protocols. The default route 0.0.0.0/0 (the gateway of last resort) matches everything but, at prefix length 0, is the least specific match and is used only when nothing more specific matches. If no route matches and no default exists, the packet is dropped.

reading.definition: By default a router forwards each packet using **longest prefix match** — the matching route with the most specific (longest) prefix. Administrative distance and metric decide which routes are installed in the table, not which matching route is used for a given packet.
keyPoints: Order: longest prefix match → administrative distance → metric.
Longest prefix match picks the MOST specific matching route and is never overridden by AD/metric.
Administrative distance compares the SAME prefix from DIFFERENT sources — lower is more trusted.
Default AD: Connected `0`, Static `1`, EIGRP `90`, OSPF `110`, RIP `120`, External EIGRP `170`, `255` = never installed.
Metric compares routes within the SAME routing protocol only — never across protocols.
Default route `0.0.0.0/0` matches everything but is least specific (used last).
reading.realWorld: On `show ip route`, the two numbers in brackets like `[110/20]` are `[AD/metric]`. A floating static route is a static route given a deliberately high AD (e.g. `ip route 10.0.0.0 255.0.0.0 10.1.1.1 130`) so it stays out of the table until the preferred OSPF/EIGRP route disappears.
commonMistakes: Thinking a lower AD can beat a more-specific (longer-prefix) route — it cannot; longest prefix match wins first.
Comparing an OSPF metric to an EIGRP metric — metrics are only comparable within one protocol.
Assuming the default route is used when a more-specific route also matches — it is the last resort only.
reading.advanced: AD is configurable per-protocol and per-static-route; this is exactly how floating static backups are built. A route with AD 255 is considered unusable and is not installed at all. Connected and local (`L`, /32) routes are added automatically when an interface is up/up.

sourceRefs.sourceName: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

questions.question: A router has routes to `10.1.1.0/24` via R2 and `10.1.1.0/26` via R3. A packet is destined for `10.1.1.10`. Which route is used?
questions.explanation: 10.1.1.10 falls inside both, but /26 is the longer (more specific) prefix, so longest prefix match selects it.

questions.question: In what order does a router apply these by default when forwarding?
choices: Longest prefix match → administrative distance → metric
questions.explanation: Longest prefix match decides which route forwards the packet; AD then metric decide which routes get installed for a given prefix.

questions.question: Two sources advertise a route to the exact same prefix: OSPF (AD 110) and a static route (AD 1). Which is installed?
choices: Neither until the tie is broken by metric
questions.explanation: For the same prefix from different sources, the lower administrative distance wins. Static (1) beats OSPF (110).

questions.question: What is the default administrative distance of a connected route?
questions.explanation: Connected routes have AD 0 — the most trusted source. Static is 1, EIGRP 90, OSPF 110, RIP 120.

questions.question: A route is learned with an administrative distance of 255. What happens?
choices: It is installed only if no metric exists
It is never installed in the routing table
It is installed but never used
questions.explanation: AD 255 means the source is considered untrustworthy; the route is not installed at all.

questions.question: True or False: A router can directly compare an OSPF metric to an EIGRP metric to pick the better route.
questions.explanation: False. Metrics are only comparable within the same routing protocol. Across protocols, administrative distance decides.

questions.question: OSPF learns two routes to `192.168.5.0/24`, one with cost 10 and one with cost 20. Which is installed (assuming no other differences)?
questions.explanation: Within one protocol, the lower metric (OSPF cost 10) wins.

questions.question: A router has `0.0.0.0/0` via R1 and `172.16.0.0/16` via R2. A packet is destined for `172.16.4.9`. Which route forwards it?
questions.explanation: The /16 matches and is far more specific than the /0 default route, so longest prefix match uses the /16.

questions.question: A packet’s destination matches no route and there is no default route. What does the router do?
choices: Sends it to the lowest-AD route
Queues it until a route appears
questions.explanation: With no matching route and no default route, the router drops the packet (and may send an ICMP unreachable).

questions.question: In `show ip route`, an entry reads `O 10.2.2.0/24 [110/30]`. What do `110` and `30` represent?
questions.explanation: The bracketed pair is `[administrative distance / metric]` — here AD 110 (OSPF) and metric 30.

questions.question: You want a static backup route used only if OSPF (AD 110) loses its route to `10.0.0.0/8`. The config shows `ip route 10.0.0.0 255.0.0.0 10.1.1.1`. Why is the backup active even while OSPF is up?
choices: Static AD (1) is lower than OSPF (110), so static is always preferred
questions.explanation: A plain static route has AD 1, beating OSPF’s 110, so it is always installed. Add a higher AD (e.g. `... 10.1.1.1 130`) to make it a floating static backup.

questions.question: Rank these by default trust (most trusted first): static, OSPF, connected.
questions.explanation: By AD: connected 0 (most trusted), static 1, OSPF 110.

flashcards.front: Which matching route does a router use to forward a packet?
flashcards.back: The one with the longest (most specific) prefix length — longest prefix match. AD/metric never override this.

flashcards.front: What does administrative distance compare?
flashcards.back: Routes to the SAME prefix from DIFFERENT sources. Lower AD = more trusted = installed.

flashcards.front: Default AD: Connected / Static / EIGRP / OSPF / RIP?
flashcards.back: 0 / 1 / 90 / 110 / 120 (External EIGRP 170; 255 = never installed).

flashcards.front: When is a routing metric used, and what is its scope?
flashcards.back: To break ties between routes to the same prefix WITHIN one routing protocol. Not comparable across protocols.

flashcards.front: Why is 0.0.0.0/0 the “last resort”?
flashcards.back: It matches every destination but has prefix length 0 — the least specific match, so it is used only when nothing more specific matches.

flashcards.front: In `[110/30]` from show ip route, which is which?
flashcards.back: [administrative distance / metric] → AD 110, metric 30.

flashcards.front: What is a floating static route?
flashcards.back: A static route given a higher-than-default AD so it stays out of the table until the preferred dynamic route is lost.

flashcards.front: Forwarding decision order (default)?
flashcards.back: Longest prefix match → administrative distance → metric.

commands.purpose: Display the IPv4 routing table, including source code, [AD/metric], next hop, and interface.
Show details for a specific route, listing the administrative distance and metric plainly.
Configure a static route; the optional trailing AD creates a floating static backup.

glossary.definition: The rule that a router forwards using the matching route with the most specific (longest) prefix length.
A 0–255 trust rating for a routing source; lower is preferred. 255 means the route is never installed.
A routing protocol’s measure of how good a route is; only comparable within the same protocol.
The 0.0.0.0/0 route (gateway of last resort) that matches any destination but is the least specific.
A static route with a raised AD so it activates only when the preferred dynamic route is lost.
The router’s chosen default route, used when no more-specific route matches.

mnemonics.title: AD default values
mnemonics.explanation: Walk the trust ladder lowest-to-highest: Connected 0, Static 1, EIGRP 90, OSPF 110, RIP 120. External EIGRP 170, and 255 = “never.”

mnemonics.title: Decision order
mnemonics.explanation: Longest Prefix match picks the route to use; Administrative Distance decides between sources; Metric mediates ties within one protocol.

examTraps.trap: Assuming the lowest AD route always wins.
examTraps.correction: AD only decides among routes to the SAME prefix. A more-specific (longer) prefix always wins first, even with a higher AD.
Metrics are protocol-specific. Across protocols, AD decides; metric is never compared between OSPF and EIGRP, etc.

examTraps.trap: Treating the default route as a normal match.
examTraps.correction: 0.0.0.0/0 is the least specific route (prefix length 0) and is used only when nothing more specific matches.
A route with AD 255 is considered unusable and is never installed in the routing table.

misconceptions.misconception: A lower metric can beat a more-specific route.
misconceptions.reality: Longest prefix match happens first and is absolute; AD and metric never override a more-specific matching route.
misconceptions.example: A /26 route is used over a /24 route for an address in both, even if the /24 has a better metric.

misconceptions.misconception: AD and metric do the same job.
misconceptions.reality: AD chooses between routing SOURCES for the same prefix; metric ranks routes WITHIN one protocol.
misconceptions.example: Static (AD 1) beats OSPF (AD 110) regardless of OSPF’s metric.

misconceptions.reality: It only forwards traffic that matches nothing more specific; if the next hop is wrong or down, traffic still fails.
misconceptions.example: A `0.0.0.0/0` pointing at a dead link drops everything not otherwise routed.

diagram.title: Longest prefix match decision
annotations: The longer prefix (/26) is more specific and wins — AD/metric are not consulted here.

sourceRefs.sourceName: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

steps.title: Match candidates
steps.action: Find every route whose prefix contains the destination IP.

steps.title: Longest prefix match
steps.action: Among matches, select the route with the longest prefix length.

steps.title: (Install-time) AD
steps.action: If two sources offered the SAME prefix, the lower-AD source was installed.

steps.title: (Install-time) Metric
steps.action: If one protocol offered several routes to that prefix, the lower metric was installed.

steps.title: Forward or drop
steps.action: Forward out the chosen route’s interface to its next hop; if nothing matched and no default exists, drop.

objectives.title: Configure and verify single area OSPFv2

ckus.title: OSPFv2
ckus.summary: A link-state IGP: routers flood LSAs to build an identical LSDB per area, then run SPF (Dijkstra) to compute shortest paths. AD 110.

ckus.title: OSPF Cost (Metric)
ckus.summary: OSPF metric = reference bandwidth (default 100 Mbps) ÷ interface bandwidth; lower cost is preferred. Total path cost sums interface costs.

ckus.title: OSPF Neighbors & RID
ckus.summary: Neighbors must match area, subnet/mask, hello/dead timers, and authentication. Router ID = highest loopback IP, else highest active interface IP, or set manually.

tiers.beginner: OSPF is a routing protocol that lets routers learn paths automatically. Each router tells the others about its links; together they build the same “map” of the network and each calculates the shortest path to every destination. Routers that share a link become “neighbors” and exchange this information. The “cost” of a path is based on link speed — faster links cost less, so OSPF prefers them.
tiers.intermediate: OSPF is a link-state IGP. Routers flood Link-State Advertisements (LSAs) to build an identical link-state database (LSDB) within an area, then run the SPF (Dijkstra) algorithm to find the lowest-cost path to each prefix. Configure with `router ospf <process-id>` then `network <addr> <wildcard> area 0` (or `ip ospf <pid> area 0` on the interface). The Router ID is the highest loopback IP, else the highest active interface IP, or set with `router-id`. Cost = reference bandwidth ÷ interface bandwidth (lower wins). Neighbors must agree on area, subnet, timers, and auth to reach Full adjacency. `passive-interface` stops hellos toward LAN hosts.
tiers.examReady: OSPFv2 = link-state IGP, AD `110`, metric = reference-bw (default 100 Mbps) ÷ interface bandwidth (lower cost wins; total = sum of egress interface costs). Config: `router ospf 1` → `network 10.0.0.0 0.0.0.255 area 0` (wildcard mask) or interface `ip ospf 1 area 0`. Router ID: highest loopback IP → highest active interface IP → manual `router-id`. Adjacency requires matching area, subnet/mask, hello/dead timers (default 10/40 on broadcast), and authentication; states Down→Init→2-Way→ExStart→Exchange→Loading→Full. On multi-access links a DR/BDR are elected (highest priority, then highest RID). `passive-interface` advertises a network but sends no hellos. Verify: `show ip ospf neighbor`, `show ip route ospf`.

reading.definition: OSPFv2 is a **link-state IGP** (AD `110`): routers flood **LSAs** into a shared **LSDB**, then run **SPF** to pick lowest-**cost** paths. Cost = reference-bw ÷ interface-bw. Neighbors must match area, subnet, timers, and auth.
keyPoints: Link-state; AD `110`; metric = reference-bw (100 Mbps) ÷ interface-bw (lower wins).
Config: `router ospf 1` → `network <addr> <wildcard> area 0`.
Router ID: highest loopback → highest active IP → manual `router-id`.
Adjacency needs matching area, subnet/mask, hello/dead timers, auth.
Neighbor states end at `Full`; DR/BDR elected on multi-access links.
`passive-interface` advertises a network but stops hellos.
reading.realWorld: Set loopbacks or a manual `router-id` for stable IDs. Default reference bandwidth treats 1G and 10G as equal cost — raise it with `auto-cost reference-bandwidth` on all routers. `show ip ospf neighbor` is the first troubleshooting stop (stuck in Init = one-way hellos; never forming = timer/subnet/area mismatch).
commonMistakes: Wrong wildcard mask in the `network` statement.
Mismatched hello/dead timers, area, or subnet preventing adjacency.
Forgetting that default reference bandwidth makes 1G+ links equal cost.
Expecting a DR/BDR on point-to-point links (there isn’t one).
reading.advanced: Single-area OSPF uses area 0. The DR reduces LSA flooding on multi-access segments by being the central point; BDR is the backup. Priority 0 means a router never becomes DR.

sourceRefs.sourceName: Cisco Press CCNA 200-301 Official Cert Guide, Vol 1 (Odom)

questions.question: What type of routing protocol is OSPF?
questions.explanation: OSPF is a link-state IGP using LSAs and SPF.

questions.question: What is OSPF’s default administrative distance?
questions.explanation: OSPF AD is 110.

questions.question: With default settings, what is the OSPF cost of a 100 Mbps link?
questions.explanation: Cost = 100 Mbps reference ÷ 100 Mbps = 1.

questions.question: How is the OSPF Router ID selected if not set manually?
choices: Highest loopback IP, else highest active interface IP
questions.explanation: Highest loopback IP wins; otherwise the highest active interface IP.

questions.question: Two OSPF routers won’t form an adjacency. Which mismatch is a likely cause?
questions.explanation: Area, subnet/mask, hello/dead timers, and auth must match to form an adjacency.

questions.question: Which command advertises 10.0.0.0/24 into OSPF area 0?
questions.explanation: OSPF `network` uses a wildcard mask: 0.0.0.255 for a /24.

questions.question: A healthy OSPF neighbor relationship settles in which state?
questions.explanation: Adjacent neighbors reach the Full state.

questions.question: What does `passive-interface` do in OSPF?
choices: Advertises the network but sends no hellos
questions.explanation: A passive interface is still advertised but sends no OSPF hellos (e.g. toward LAN hosts).

questions.question: On a multi-access Ethernet segment, how is the DR elected?
choices: Highest OSPF priority, then highest Router ID
questions.explanation: Highest priority wins; ties break on highest Router ID. Priority 0 never becomes DR.

flashcards.front: OSPF type and AD?
flashcards.back: Link-state IGP; administrative distance 110.

flashcards.front: OSPF cost formula?
flashcards.back: Reference bandwidth (default 100 Mbps) ÷ interface bandwidth; lower cost wins.

flashcards.front: Router ID selection order?
flashcards.back: Highest loopback IP → highest active interface IP → manual router-id.

flashcards.front: What must match to form an adjacency?
flashcards.back: Area, subnet/mask, hello/dead timers, authentication.

flashcards.front: Command to advertise a /24 into area 0?
flashcards.back: network <addr> 0.0.0.255 area 0 (wildcard mask).

flashcards.front: Final neighbor state + verify command?
flashcards.back: Full; show ip ospf neighbor.

commands.purpose: Enter OSPF configuration for a process.
Advertise matching interfaces into an OSPF area.
Show OSPF neighbors and their adjacency state.

glossary.definition: Open Shortest Path First — a link-state interior gateway protocol (AD 110).
Link-State Advertisement; the database of LSAs all routers in an area share.
Metric = reference bandwidth ÷ interface bandwidth; lower is preferred.
A 32-bit ID for an OSPF router (highest loopback, else interface IP, or manual).
Designated/Backup Designated Router that reduce LSA flooding on multi-access links.

mnemonics.title: Neighbor match
mnemonics.explanation: Four things that must match to form an OSPF adjacency.

mnemonics.title: Cost
mnemonics.explanation: OSPF cost = reference bandwidth divided by interface bandwidth.

examTraps.trap: Using a subnet mask in the network statement.
examTraps.correction: OSPF `network` uses a WILDCARD mask (0.0.0.255 for /24).

examTraps.trap: Assuming 1G and 10G have different OSPF cost by default.
examTraps.correction: Default reference bandwidth (100 Mbps) caps cost at 1, making them equal — raise the reference bandwidth.
DR/BDR are only elected on multi-access (e.g. Ethernet) segments.

misconceptions.misconception: OSPF uses hop count like RIP.
misconceptions.reality: OSPF uses cost (bandwidth-based), not hop count.
misconceptions.example: A 2-hop fast path can beat a 1-hop slow path.

misconceptions.reality: Lower cost is preferred — it represents a faster path.
misconceptions.example: A 1G link (cost 1 by default) beats a slower link.

steps.title: Hello
steps.action: Routers send hellos; matching parameters reach 2-Way.

steps.title: Exchange
steps.action: Neighbors exchange database descriptions (ExStart/Exchange).

steps.title: Loading
steps.action: Each requests missing LSAs to sync the LSDB.

steps.title: Full
steps.action: LSDBs match; adjacency is Full and SPF runs.

objectives.title: Interpret the components of a routing table

ckus.title: Routing Table Entry Structure
ckus.summary: Each entry shows: source code (C/S/O/D/R/B), destination prefix, [AD/metric] in brackets, next-hop IP, outgoing interface, and age timer for dynamic routes.

ckus.title: Route Source Codes
ckus.summary: C=connected AD 0, L=local /32 AD 0, S=static AD 1, O=OSPF AD 110, D=EIGRP AD 90, R=RIP AD 120, B=BGP. The code tells you how the router learned the route.

ckus.title: Connected and Local Routes
ckus.summary: When an interface is up/up with an IP, the router auto-installs: a C route to the subnet and an L /32 for the router own interface IP. Both have AD 0 and are removed when the interface fails.

tiers.beginner: The routing table is a list of destinations the router knows how to reach. Each row shows how the router learned the route (a letter code like C for connected, S for static, O for OSPF), the destination network, the next router to forward to (next-hop IP), and which interface to exit. When you set up an IP address on an interface it is automatically added.
tiers.intermediate: Each routing table entry contains: a source code, the destination network/prefix in CIDR notation, the [AD/metric] pair in brackets, the next-hop IP and outgoing interface, and an age timer for dynamic routes. Connected (C) routes and local (L) /32 routes are added automatically when an interface reaches up/up state; they have AD 0 and disappear if the interface fails. The longest prefix match rule selects which route is used for a given packet.
tiers.examReady: Entry anatomy: O 192.168.2.0/24 [110/20] via 10.1.1.1, 00:01:23, Gi0/0 means: O=OSPF source, /24 destination, AD=110 metric=20, next-hop 10.1.1.1, age 1m23s, exit Gi0/0. Source codes: C=connected AD 0, L=local /32 AD 0, S=static AD 1, O=OSPF AD 110, D=EIGRP AD 90, R=RIP AD 120, B=BGP. When an interface comes up/up with an IP, the router installs both a C route (subnet) and an L route (exact /32 for the router own interface IP). These vanish when the interface goes down. An asterisk (*) marks the best candidate. The "gateway of last resort" at the top shows the default route.

reading.definition: A routing table entry records the **destination prefix**, source **code** (C/L/S/O/D/R/B), **[AD/metric]** trustworthiness/cost pair, **next-hop** IP or exit interface, and age.
keyPoints: Source codes: `C`=connected (AD 0), `L`=local /32 (AD 0), `S`=static (AD 1), `O`=OSPF (110), `D`=EIGRP (90), `R`=RIP (120).
Brackets `[AD/metric]` — e.g. `[110/20]` = AD 110, metric 20. AD comes first.
When Gi0/1 comes up with an IP, C and L routes auto-install; both removed if interface fails.
The L route is a /32 for the router own interface IP — lets the router process traffic addressed to itself.
An asterisk (*) marks the best current match for a prefix or the candidate default route.
reading.realWorld: Run `show ip route`. The header shows the gateway of last resort. A line like `O 10.2.0.0/24 [110/2] via 10.0.0.1, 00:05:10, Gi0/1` means OSPF learned this, AD 110, cost 2, forward to 10.0.0.1 out Gi0/1, updated 5 min ago.
commonMistakes: Confusing the L (/32) route for a host route to a PC — it is the router own interface address.
Thinking [AD/metric] is [metric/AD] — it is always AD first, metric second.
Forgetting that connected routes disappear when the interface goes down.
related: 3.2 Forwarding decision (longest prefix match, AD, metric)
reading.advanced: Run `show ip route summary` for counts by type. For IPv6 use `show ipv6 route`. Local (L) routes for the router own IPs are essential so the router responds to pings and SSH targeted at itself.

questions.question: In `show ip route`, what does the source code `O` represent?
questions.explanation: O = OSPF. C = connected, S = static, D = EIGRP.

questions.question: A routing entry shows `[110/20]`. What does 110 represent?
questions.explanation: Format is [AD/metric]. 110 is the administrative distance (OSPF), 20 is the metric (OSPF cost).

questions.question: When an interface comes up with IP 192.168.1.1/24, which two routes are auto-installed?
questions.explanation: A connected (C) route to the subnet and a local (L) /32 for the router own interface IP are both auto-installed at AD 0.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the L (local) /32 route in the routing table?
choices: It routes traffic to hosts on the subnet
It allows the router to process packets destined for its own interface IP
It is a backup for the connected route
questions.explanation: The L /32 lets the router recognize and process packets addressed directly to its own interface IP (e.g. for SSH or ping to the router itself).

questions.question: Which source code indicates a route learned via EIGRP?
questions.explanation: D = EIGRP (Diffusing Update Algorithm). O = OSPF, R = RIP, B = BGP.

questions.question: Interface Gi0/1 goes down. What happens to its connected route?
questions.explanation: Connected and local routes are removed when the interface fails. They cannot be reached directly anymore.

questions.question: What is the default administrative distance of a static route?
questions.explanation: Static routes have AD 1 by default — one higher than connected (0) but lower than any dynamic protocol.

questions.question: A route reads: `D 172.16.0.0/16 [90/307200] via 10.0.0.2, 00:10:00, Gi0/0`. What is 307200?
questions.explanation: D = EIGRP. [90/307200] = [AD/metric]. 307200 is the EIGRP composite metric (bandwidth + delay). AD is 90.

questions.question: The top of `show ip route` shows "Gateway of last resort is 203.0.113.1". What does this indicate?
choices: The router cannot reach any network
The router is a DHCP server
questions.explanation: The gateway of last resort message means a default route (0.0.0.0/0) is installed, pointing to 203.0.113.1.

questions.question: An asterisk (*) next to a routing entry indicates what?
choices: It is the best match or candidate default
The route is more than 24 hours old
questions.explanation: An asterisk marks the route as the best path for that prefix or indicates it as the candidate default route (for the gateway of last resort).

flashcards.front: Route source code C in `show ip route`?
flashcards.back: Connected — auto-installed when an interface is up/up. AD 0.

flashcards.front: Route source code L in `show ip route`?
flashcards.back: Local — a /32 for the router own interface IP. AD 0. Used to process traffic destined for the router itself.

flashcards.front: CCNA route codes: C / L / S / O / D / R?
flashcards.back: C=connected, L=local, S=static, O=OSPF, D=EIGRP, R=RIP (B=BGP, i=IS-IS).

flashcards.front: What do `[110/20]` mean in a routing entry?
flashcards.back: [AD/metric] — AD 110 (OSPF), metric (cost) 20.

flashcards.front: Two routes auto-added when Gi0/1 comes up with 10.1.1.1/24?
flashcards.back: C 10.1.1.0/24 (connected subnet) and L 10.1.1.1/32 (local). Both removed if interface fails.

flashcards.front: Gateway of last resort in `show ip route`?
flashcards.back: The next-hop for the default route (0.0.0.0/0) — used when no more-specific route matches.

diagram.title: Routing Table Entry Anatomy
annotations: C and L routes have no [AD/metric] shown — they are always AD 0
L routes are /32 for the router own IPs

commands.purpose: Display the full IPv4 routing table with source codes, [AD/metric], next-hop, and interface.
Show details for a specific destination prefix, including which route was selected.
Filter the routing table to connected (C) routes only.

glossary.definition: A letter (C/L/S/O/D/R/B) indicating how the router learned each route.
A route automatically installed when an interface is up/up with an IP — AD 0.
A /32 route for the router own interface IP — lets it process traffic destined to itself.
The default route (0.0.0.0/0) used when no more-specific route matches.

mnemonics.title: Route codes
mnemonics.mnemonic: C-L-S-O-D-R — Connected, Local, Static, OSPF, EIGRP, RIP.
mnemonics.explanation: The first letter of each entry tells you the routing source at a glance.

mnemonics.title: Bracket order
mnemonics.explanation: Never read [20/110] backwards; AD always precedes metric in Cisco output.

examTraps.trap: The L route is NOT a host route to a PC.
examTraps.correction: L is the router own interface IP as a /32 — so the router processes packets addressed to itself.
Many students read it backwards as [metric/AD]. AD is always the first number inside the brackets.

misconceptions.misconception: The L route forwards traffic to end hosts.
misconceptions.reality: L routes exist only for the router own interface IPs — not for PCs or servers.
misconceptions.example: L 10.1.1.1/32 lets R1 accept SSH to 10.1.1.1, not route to a host.

misconceptions.misconception: Connected routes persist after an interface goes down.
misconceptions.reality: C and L routes are removed immediately when the interface fails.
misconceptions.example: Shut down Gi0/1 and its C/L entries vanish from the table.

steps.title: Source code
steps.action: Read the leading letter (C/S/O/D) to identify how the route was learned.

steps.title: Prefix
steps.action: Note the destination network and prefix length.

steps.title: AD/metric
steps.action: Parse [AD/metric] — trust level then protocol cost.

steps.title: Next-hop / interface
steps.action: Identify via next-hop IP and outgoing interface for forwarding.

objectives.title: Configure and verify IPv4 and IPv6 static routing

ckus.title: IPv4 Static Route Syntax
ckus.summary: ip route <dest-network> <subnet-mask> {<next-hop-ip> | <exit-interface>} [AD]. Using a next-hop IP is preferred over exit-interface alone on multi-access Ethernet segments.

ckus.title: Default Static Route
ckus.summary: ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop> creates the gateway of last resort. Matches any destination when no more-specific route exists. Common on stub/edge routers connected to ISPs.

ckus.title: Floating Static Route
ckus.summary: A static route with an AD higher than the primary dynamic route (e.g. AD 130 to back up OSPF AD 110). Not installed while the primary route is present; rises in when the primary fails.

ckus.title: IPv6 Static Route Syntax
ckus.summary: ipv6 route <prefix>/<length> {<next-hop-ipv6> | <exit-interface>}. IPv6 default: ipv6 route ::/0 <next-hop>. Requires ipv6 unicast-routing globally.

tiers.beginner: A static route tells the router "to reach network X, forward to Y". They are simple to set up but must be manually updated when the network changes. Use `ip route` with a destination network, its mask, and the next-hop address. A default static route is a catch-all used when no other route matches.
tiers.intermediate: Syntax: `ip route <destination-network> <subnet-mask> <next-hop-ip-or-interface>`. Prefer next-hop IP over exit-interface on Ethernet so the router does not ARP for every destination. Default static: `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop>`. Floating static: add AD > primary protocol AD (e.g. 130 for OSPF backup) — only installs when OSPF route disappears. IPv6: `ipv6 unicast-routing` first, then `ipv6 route <prefix>/<len> <next-hop>`, default is `ipv6 route ::/0 <next-hop>`. Verify: `show ip route static`, `ping`.
tiers.examReady: IPv4 static: `ip route 10.0.0.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.1` — installs S 10.0.0.0/24 [1/0] via 192.168.1.1. Default static: `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 203.0.113.1`. Floating static: `ip route 10.0.0.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.2.1 130` — AD 130 stays out while OSPF (AD 110) is active; installed when OSPF loses the route. Recursive lookup: next-hop IP must itself be in the routing table (via connected or other route) or the static is not installed. IPv6: `ipv6 unicast-routing` required first, then `ipv6 route 2001:db8::/32 2001:db8::1`, default `ipv6 route ::/0 <next-hop>`. Verify: `show ip route static`, `show ipv6 route static`, `ping`.

reading.definition: A **static route** is a manually configured forwarding entry. Syntax: `ip route <network> <mask> {<next-hop> | <interface>}`. A **default** uses 0.0.0.0/0. A **floating** static adds a higher AD to serve as a dynamic-route backup.
keyPoints: `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop>` — default route, gateway of last resort.
Floating static: set AD > primary protocol AD so it only installs when the primary route fails.
IPv6: enable `ipv6 unicast-routing` first; then `ipv6 route <prefix>/<len> <next-hop>`.
Recursive lookup: the next-hop IP must be reachable or the static route is not installed.
Verify with `show ip route static` and ping from the correct source interface.
reading.realWorld: On a branch router: `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 203.0.113.1` points all internet traffic to the ISP. For a floating backup: `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 198.51.100.1 130` — AD 130 beats OSPF is wrong (130>110 means OSPF wins). Wait — 130 > 110 so OSPF (lower AD) stays preferred. Only when OSPF disappears does the floating static at AD 130 install.
commonMistakes: Using exit interface without next-hop IP on Ethernet — the router ARP-resolves every destination IP inefficiently.
Setting a floating static AD lower than the dynamic protocol AD — it becomes primary, not a backup.
Forgetting `ipv6 unicast-routing` before IPv6 static routes.
related: 3.1 Routing table (where static routes appear as S)
reading.advanced: Recursive static: the router looks up the next-hop in its own table — if that fails the static is not installed. Use `show ip route 10.0.0.0` to check if a specific prefix is installed.

questions.question: Which command adds a static route to 172.16.0.0/16 via next-hop 10.0.0.1?
questions.explanation: ip route <network> <subnet-mask> <next-hop>. /16 = 255.255.0.0.

questions.question: Which command creates a default static route via 203.0.113.1?
questions.explanation: Default static route: destination 0.0.0.0 mask 0.0.0.0 (/0) matches all traffic.

questions.question: OSPF (AD 110) is primary. Which config makes a static route a floating backup?
questions.explanation: AD 130 is higher than OSPF AD 110, so the static is only installed when OSPF loses the route. AD 90 would always beat OSPF.

questions.question: A static route is configured but not in `show ip route`. The next-hop is in a disconnected subnet. Why?
choices: Static routes require OSPF to advertise them
The next-hop must be reachable — recursive lookup fails
Static routes must use exit interface only
questions.explanation: A static route requires its next-hop to be reachable via another routing table entry. If not reachable, the static is not installed.

questions.question: Which command configures a static IPv6 route to 2001:db8:1::/48 via 2001:db8::1?
questions.explanation: IPv6 static: `ipv6 route <prefix>/<len> <next-hop>`. Requires `ipv6 unicast-routing` globally.

questions.question: What is the IPv6 equivalent of `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 <next-hop>`?
questions.explanation: IPv6 default route is ::/0 (prefix length 0 matches everything).

questions.question: Which command shows only static routes in the routing table?
questions.explanation: `show ip route static` filters to entries with source code S.

questions.question: A static route uses only an exit interface (Gi0/0) on a multi-access Ethernet. What is the issue?
choices: The route disappears from the table
The router ARP-resolves every destination individually, wasting resources
The router uses OSPF to resolve the path
questions.explanation: On Ethernet without a next-hop IP, the router treats every destination as directly connected and ARP-resolves each one — inefficient on multi-access segments.

flashcards.front: IPv4 static route to 10.1.0.0/24 via 192.168.1.1?
Default static route via 203.0.113.1?
flashcards.back: `ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 203.0.113.1` — sets the gateway of last resort.

flashcards.front: How to make a static backup for OSPF (AD 110)?
flashcards.back: Set AD higher than 110: `ip route ... <next-hop> 130` — installed only when OSPF loses the route.

flashcards.front: IPv6 default static route?
flashcards.back: `ipv6 route ::/0 <next-hop>` (after `ipv6 unicast-routing`).

flashcards.front: Static not in routing table — most likely reason?
flashcards.back: Next-hop is not reachable (recursive lookup failed). No connected route to the next-hop subnet.

diagram.title: Static vs Floating Static Route
annotations: Floating static (AD 130) only installs if OSPF (AD 110) route disappears

commands.purpose: Configure an IPv4 static route to a destination via next-hop IP.
Configure a default static route (gateway of last resort).
Configure an IPv6 static route (requires ipv6 unicast-routing globally).

glossary.definition: A manually configured routing table entry — AD 1 by default.
0.0.0.0/0 (or ::/0) — matches any destination when nothing more specific exists.
A static route with raised AD that installs only when the primary dynamic route fails.
The router must resolve the next-hop IP via another route before installing the static.

mnemonics.title: Default route
mnemonics.explanation: Both network and mask are all zeros for the IPv4 default route.

mnemonics.title: Floating static
mnemonics.mnemonic: Float ABOVE — AD higher than the protocol you are backing up.
mnemonics.explanation: To back up OSPF (110), use AD > 110 such as 130.

examTraps.trap: A floating static needs AD HIGHER than the dynamic protocol.
examTraps.correction: Setting AD 90 to back up OSPF (AD 110) would always be preferred — it becomes primary, not backup.

examTraps.trap: IPv6 static routes require `ipv6 unicast-routing` globally first.
examTraps.correction: Without `ipv6 unicast-routing`, IPv6 static routes are not installed — a common exam gotcha.

misconceptions.misconception: Exit-interface static routes are always preferred on Ethernet.
misconceptions.reality: Next-hop IP is preferred on multi-access segments to avoid ARP for every destination.
misconceptions.example: ip route 10.0.0.0 255.0.0.0 Gi0/0 causes ARP storms; use a next-hop IP instead.

misconceptions.misconception: A static route installs even if the next-hop is unreachable.
misconceptions.reality: Recursive lookup must succeed — the next-hop must be reachable via another route.
misconceptions.example: Static via 10.99.0.1 fails if no route to 10.99.0.0/24 exists.

steps.title: Configure
steps.action: Enter `ip route` with destination, mask, and next-hop or interface.

steps.title: Recursive lookup
steps.action: Router verifies the next-hop is reachable in the routing table.

steps.title: Install
steps.action: Route appears as S in `show ip route` with AD 1 (or custom AD).

steps.title: Verify
steps.action: Ping a host in the destination subnet from the correct source.

lab.title: Configure Single-Area OSPFv2 Between Two Routers
lab.scenario: R1 and R2 are connected via a point-to-point link and each have a LAN behind them. You will configure IP addressing, enable OSPFv2 in area 0 on both routers, and verify the neighbor adjacency forms and both LANs appear in each router's routing table.
learningGoals: Configure interface IP addressing for a point-to-point link and a LAN.
Enable OSPFv2 with a router ID and area 0 network statements.
Verify OSPF routes appear in the routing table with AD 110.

tasks.title: Addressing on R1
tasks.instruction: Configure R1: Gi0/0 = 10.0.12.1/30 (link to R2), Gi0/1 = 10.0.1.1/24 (LAN1). Bring both interfaces up.

tasks.title: Addressing on R2
tasks.instruction: Configure R2: Gi0/0 = 10.0.12.2/30 (link to R1), Gi0/1 = 10.0.2.1/24 (LAN2). Bring both interfaces up.

tasks.title: Enable OSPF on R1
tasks.instruction: Start OSPF process 1 with router-id 1.1.1.1, and advertise both R1 networks into area 0.

tasks.title: Enable OSPF on R2
tasks.instruction: Start OSPF process 1 with router-id 2.2.2.2, and advertise both R2 networks into area 0.

tasks.title: Verify adjacency
tasks.instruction: Confirm R1 and R2 have formed a FULL OSPF neighbor adjacency.

tasks.title: Verify routes
tasks.instruction: Confirm R1's routing table contains an OSPF (O) route to R2's LAN (10.0.2.0/24).

successCriteria: show ip ospf neighbor shows R2 (or R1) in state FULL.
show ip route ospf on R1 shows O 10.0.2.0/24 via 10.0.12.2.
show ip route ospf on R2 shows O 10.0.1.0/24 via 10.0.12.1.
Routes show administrative distance 110 (O).
A PC on LAN1 can ping a PC on LAN2.
failureCriteria: Mismatched area numbers on the two ends of a link → neighbors never form.
Wrong wildcard mask on the network statement → the link or LAN is not advertised.
Mismatched hello/dead timers → adjacency stuck in a non-FULL state.
Interface left administratively down (`shutdown`) → no adjacency at all.
commonMistakes: Using a subnet mask instead of a wildcard mask in the `network` command.
Forgetting `no shutdown` on newly configured interfaces.
Putting the two ends of the same link in different OSPF areas.
Expecting routes to appear before the neighbor adjacency reaches FULL.

topology.title: Single-area OSPF topology
notes: Both routers are in OSPF area 0 (single-area design).

failureChecks.reason: Mismatched area, wildcard mask, or a shutdown interface prevents the adjacency from forming.

diagram.title: OSPF neighbor adjacency and route exchange
annotations: Both routers must agree on area, subnet/mask, and hello/dead timers to become neighbors.
After LSDB exchange, the adjacency reaches FULL and SPF computes routes.

steps.title: Hello
steps.action: R1 and R2 send Hello packets out the shared link.

steps.title: Parameter check
steps.action: Each checks the other's area ID, subnet/mask, and hello/dead timers match.

steps.title: Database exchange
steps.action: Routers exchange link-state databases (LSDB).

steps.title: FULL
steps.action: Adjacency reaches FULL; SPF runs and routes are installed.

steps.title: LSA advertised
steps.action: R2 advertises 10.0.2.0/24 via an LSA.

steps.title: SPF run
steps.action: R1 runs SPF and computes the lowest-cost path to 10.0.2.0/24 via R2.

lab.title: Configure Static Routes with a Floating Static Backup
lab.scenario: R1 and R2 each host a LAN and are connected by two links: a primary (Gi0/0) and a backup (Gi0/1). You will configure a normal static route over the primary link, and a FLOATING static route over the backup link with a higher administrative distance so it is only used if the primary link fails.
learningGoals: Configure a static route to a remote network via a next-hop IP.
Configure a floating static route with a non-default administrative distance.
Verify which route is preferred (lowest AD) in the routing table.
Verify automatic failover to the floating static route when the primary link goes down.

tasks.title: Addressing on R1
tasks.instruction: Configure R1: Gi0/2 = 10.0.1.1/24 (LAN1), Gi0/0 = 10.0.12.1/30 (primary link), Gi0/1 = 10.0.13.1/30 (backup link). Bring all up.

tasks.title: Addressing on R2
tasks.instruction: Configure R2: Gi0/2 = 10.0.2.1/24 (LAN2), Gi0/0 = 10.0.12.2/30 (primary link), Gi0/1 = 10.0.13.2/30 (backup link). Bring all up.

tasks.title: Primary static route on R1
tasks.instruction: Add a static route to LAN2 (10.0.2.0/24) via the primary link next-hop 10.0.12.2 (default AD 1).

tasks.title: Floating static on R1
tasks.instruction: Add a FLOATING static route to LAN2 via the backup link next-hop 10.0.13.2, with administrative distance 5 so it is only used if the primary route disappears.

tasks.title: Primary + floating static on R2
tasks.instruction: Mirror the routes on R2 toward LAN1 (10.0.1.0/24): primary via 10.0.12.1, floating via 10.0.13.1 with AD 5.

tasks.title: Verify and test failover
tasks.instruction: Confirm the primary route (AD 1) is installed and preferred. Then shut Gi0/0 and confirm the floating static (AD 5) is installed instead.

successCriteria: show ip route static on R1 shows S 10.0.2.0/24 [1/0] via 10.0.12.2 as the active route.
The floating static (S 10.0.2.0/24 [5/0] via 10.0.13.2) exists but is NOT in the routing table while the primary is up.
After `shutdown` on Gi0/0, the primary route disappears and the floating static (AD 5) is installed and becomes active.
A PC on LAN1 can still reach LAN2 after the primary link fails (via the backup).
failureCriteria: Both static routes given the same AD → both attempt to install, causing load-balancing instead of backup-only behavior.
Floating static AD lower than or equal to a dynamic routing protocol's AD → it could be preferred over a better dynamic route.
Next-hop IP typo → route never installs (recursive lookup fails).
Forgetting `no shutdown` on new interfaces → routes reference a down interface and never install.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the AD number entirely on the floating static — without it, AD defaults to 1, same as the primary, and both compete.
Confusing AD 5 (a static route's floating distance) with OSPF's AD 110 — floating statics are usually given an AD lower than dynamic protocols but higher than the primary static (1).
Testing failover without actually shutting the primary interface — the floating route never appears while the primary is reachable.
Writing the destination network/mask incorrectly so the static route never matches the LAN2 traffic.

topology.title: Static routing with primary + backup links
notes: Primary static route has AD 1 (default) and is preferred.
Floating static (AD 5) is only installed if the primary route is removed.

verificationChecks.expectedResult: After shutting Gi0/0, S 10.0.2.0/24 [5/0] via 10.0.13.2 becomes active.

failureChecks.expectedFailure: Floating static missing AD or shares AD 1 with the primary
failureChecks.reason: Both routes would attempt to install, causing unwanted load-balancing instead of backup-only behavior.

diagram.title: Floating static activates on primary failure
annotations: When Gi0/0 (primary) goes down, R1 removes the AD-1 static route.
With no competing route, the AD-5 floating static is installed and traffic now flows over the backup link.

steps.title: Both routes configured
steps.action: R1 has a primary static (AD 1) and a floating static (AD 5) to 10.0.2.0/24.

steps.title: Best AD wins
steps.action: The router installs only the lowest-AD route — the primary (AD 1).

steps.title: Forward
steps.action: Traffic to LAN2 is forwarded via 10.0.12.2 (primary).

steps.title: Primary link down
steps.action: Gi0/0 is shut down (or fails); the AD-1 static route is removed from the table.

steps.title: Floating static installed
steps.action: With no AD-1 route present, the AD-5 floating static to 10.0.2.0/24 is installed.

steps.title: Forward via backup
steps.action: Traffic to LAN2 now flows via 10.0.13.2 (backup link).

lab.title: Configure HSRP Gateway Redundancy
lab.scenario: Two routers share a LAN segment. R1 should be the active HSRP gateway (priority 150 + preempt); R2 is the standby (default priority 100). Virtual IP is 192.168.1.1/24 on Gi0/0.

tasks.title: Address R1 Gi0/0
tasks.instruction: Configure Gi0/0 with 192.168.1.2/24 and bring it up.

tasks.title: HSRP active on R1
tasks.instruction: Enable HSRP group 1 with virtual IP 192.168.1.1, priority 150, and preempt.

tasks.title: Address R2 Gi0/0
tasks.instruction: Configure Gi0/0 with 192.168.1.3/24 and bring it up.

tasks.title: HSRP standby on R2
tasks.instruction: Join HSRP group 1 with the same virtual IP 192.168.1.1 (default priority 100).

tasks.title: Verify roles
tasks.instruction: Confirm R1 is Active and R2 is Standby.

successCriteria: R1 shows Active state for group 1
R2 shows Standby state
Virtual IP 192.168.1.1 is reachable by LAN hosts
failureCriteria: Missing preempt on R1 — R1 may not reclaim Active after recovery
Mismatched group or virtual IP — no adjacency
commonMistakes: Forgetting preempt on the higher-priority router
Different standby group numbers on each router
Virtual IP not in the same subnet as physical interfaces

steps.title: Host ARP
steps.action: Host ARPs for default gateway 192.168.1.1

steps.title: Active forwards
steps.action: R1 (Active) responds and forwards traffic

lab.title: Troubleshoot OSPF Area Mismatch
lab.scenario: Symptom: R1 and R2 are connected on 10.0.12.0/30 but `show ip ospf neighbor` shows no FULL adjacency. R2 was misconfigured with network 10.0.12.0 in area 1 instead of area 0. Fix R2 so both routers are in area 0.
learningGoals: Use show commands to isolate fault

tasks.title: Gather data
tasks.instruction: Run show ip ospf neighbor and show run | section router ospf to find the area mismatch.

tasks.title: Fix area on R2
tasks.instruction: Re-advertise 10.0.12.0/30 into area 0 (not area 1).

tasks.title: Verify adjacency
tasks.instruction: Confirm FULL neighbor state.

successCriteria: Symptom resolved after fix commands entered
failureCriteria: Fix applied on wrong interface or wrong direction
commonMistakes: Wildcard mask off by one bit
Network statement in wrong area

steps.title: Symptom
steps.action: Symptom: R1 and R2 are connected on 10.0.12.0/30 but `show ip ospf neighbor` sho

steps.title: Fix
steps.action: Correct command restores expected behavior

lab.title: Troubleshoot Native VLAN Mismatch
lab.scenario: Symptom: CDP reports native VLAN mismatch between SW1 and SW2 on the Gi0/1 trunk. SW1 uses native VLAN 1; SW2 was set to native VLAN 99. Align SW2 to VLAN 1.
learningGoals: Use show commands to isolate fault

tasks.title: Inspect CDP
tasks.instruction: Use show cdp neighbors detail to read the native VLAN mismatch warning.

tasks.title: Fix SW2 native VLAN
tasks.instruction: On Gi0/1 trunk, set native VLAN back to 1 to match SW1.

tasks.title: Verify trunk
tasks.instruction: Confirm trunk encapsulation and native VLAN.

successCriteria: Symptom resolved after fix commands entered
failureCriteria: Fix applied on wrong interface or wrong direction
commonMistakes: Changing allowed VLAN list instead of native VLAN

steps.title: Symptom
steps.action: Symptom: CDP reports native VLAN mismatch between SW1 and SW2 on the Gi0/1 trunk

steps.title: Fix
steps.action: Correct command restores expected behavior

lab.title: Troubleshoot Shutdown Interface
lab.scenario: Symptom: PC1 cannot ping its gateway 192.168.1.1 on R1. `show ip interface brief` shows Gi0/0 administratively down. Bring the interface up.
learningGoals: Use show commands to isolate fault

tasks.title: Identify down interface
tasks.instruction: Run show ip interface brief — Gi0/0 is administratively down.

tasks.title: Enable interface
tasks.instruction: Enter interface Gi0/0 and issue no shutdown.

tasks.title: Test connectivity
tasks.instruction: Ping the gateway 192.168.1.1.

successCriteria: Symptom resolved after fix commands entered
failureCriteria: Fix applied on wrong interface or wrong direction
commonMistakes: Fixing IP address when the issue is simply shutdown

steps.title: Symptom
steps.action: Symptom: PC1 cannot ping its gateway 192.168.1.1 on R1. `show ip interface brief

steps.title: Fix
steps.action: Correct command restores expected behavior

lab.title: Troubleshoot Missing Default Route
lab.scenario: Symptom: R1 LAN hosts reach local subnets but cannot reach internet 198.51.100.1. R1 has no default route. Add ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 203.0.113.1 on R1.
learningGoals: Use show commands to isolate fault

tasks.title: Check routing table
tasks.instruction: show ip route — no gateway of last resort.

tasks.title: Add default route
tasks.instruction: Point default traffic to ISP next-hop 203.0.113.1.

tasks.title: Verify

successCriteria: Symptom resolved after fix commands entered
failureCriteria: Fix applied on wrong interface or wrong direction
commonMistakes: Static route to wrong next-hop

steps.action: Symptom: R1 LAN hosts reach local subnets but cannot reach internet 198.51.100.1

steps.title: Fix

lab.title: Troubleshoot HSRP Active on Wrong Router
lab.scenario: Symptom: R2 (lower priority) is Active and R1 (intended primary) is Standby. R1 is missing preempt. On R1, add standby 1 preempt and standby 1 priority 150.
learningGoals: Use show commands to isolate fault

tasks.title: Check standby roles
tasks.instruction: show standby brief — R2 is Active unexpectedly.

tasks.title: Fix R1 priority/preempt
tasks.instruction: Set priority 150 and enable preempt on group 1.

tasks.title: Confirm failover
tasks.instruction: Verify R1 becomes Active.

lab.title: Configure IPv6 Addressing and Static Routes
lab.scenario: R1 connects to R2 on 2001:db8:12::/64. R2 has LAN 2001:db8:20::/64. Enable IPv6 routing on both routers, assign interface addresses, and add static routes so R1 reaches the remote LAN.

tasks.title: Enable IPv6 routing R1
tasks.instruction: Enable ipv6 unicast-routing on R1.

tasks.title: Verify
tasks.instruction: Show IPv6 route table.

successCriteria: IPv6 static route installed
Reachability to remote /64
commonMistakes: Forgetting ipv6 unicast-routing
Using link-local as static next-hop without understanding scope

lab.title: Advertise a Default Route with OSPF
lab.scenario: R1 is the edge router with Internet default via 203.0.113.1. R2 is internal. Configure OSPF on both, then originate default route from R1 into OSPF area 0 so R2 learns 0.0.0.0/0.
learningGoals: Verify O E2 default on R2

tasks.title: Default static R1
tasks.instruction: Add default route to ISP.

tasks.title: OSPF R1
tasks.instruction: Enable OSPF 1, advertise LAN and link into area 0.

tasks.title: Originate default
tasks.instruction: Inside OSPF, default-information originate.

tasks.title: OSPF R2
tasks.instruction: Enable OSPF on R2 for shared link and LAN.

tasks.title: Verify R2
tasks.instruction: Confirm O*E2 default in routing table.

successCriteria: R2 receives OSPF external default
R1 originates only with existing default/static
commonMistakes: originate without default route present
Area mismatch prevents learning default

topology.title: Advertise a Default Route with OSPF

diagram.title: Advertise a Default Route with OSPF

packetFlows.title: Advertise a Default Route with OSPF

steps.title: Edge default
steps.action: R1 has static default to ISP

steps.title: Redistribute
steps.action: OSPF advertises default into area 0
